analysis_summary: The proposed method produced 8 finding(s) from 8 analysis obligation(s).

findings.summary: Debug APB path can halt/resume the core, write debug settings, request PC jump, write GPRs, and access CSRs without any observed authentication/authorization guard; use-site guards only require debug_halted_o for internal GPR/CSR/DNPC accesses, while DBG_CTRL is always accessible and can initiate halt/resume.
findings.vulnerability_category: read/write/halt/resume/jump/register/CSR access through debug path / CPU architectural state: GPR register file, CSRs, debug settings, DNPC/jump target, core halt/resume state / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: external/APB debug requester via apb_slave_debug and apb2per_debug_i Operation: read/write/halt/resume/jump/register/CSR access through debug path Object: CPU architectural state: GPR register file, CSRs, debug settings, DNPC/jump target, core halt/resume state Expected guard: debug authentication/authorization and debug-mode entry/enable gating before any debug read/write/override of CPU architectural state Observed behavior: fc_subsystem lines 294-320 translate APB debug slave transactions directly to debug_req/debug_addr/debug_we/debug_wdata. zeroriscy_debug_unit ports expose debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i, debug_halt_i/debug_resume_i, regfile_rreq/wreq, csr_req/we and jump_req. In debug_unit lines 140-159 CSR writes latch/issue csr_req/csr_we when halted; lines 163-182 mark debug registers always accessible and DBG_CTRL can set dbg_halt/dbg_resume; lines 196-204 DNPC jump request is granted and only actioned if halted; lines 207-212 GPR write request is granted and only actioned if halted; line 215 grants invalid/default accesses to avoid blocking. Path: fc_subsystem apb_slave_debug -> apb2per_debug_i -> debug_req/debug_addr/debug_we/debug_wdata -> zeroriscy_core debug interface -> zeroriscy_debug_unit -> regfile/csr/jump/stall/control signals Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: An APB-accessible debug requester that can reach apb_slave_debug can force core halt/resume and, after halt, read/write GPRs/CSRs or redirect PC without an authentication/lifecycle/debug-enable check in the inspected path.

findings.summary: CSR module updates machine/status/exception CSRs based on csr_access_i/csr_op_i-derived write enable and address decode, with no observed privilege/access-class check or illegal-access response in the inspected evidence.
findings.vulnerability_category: CSR read/write/set/clear / mstatus, mepc, mcause and performance/control CSRs in zeroriscy_cs_registers / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: CPU instruction stream/software and debug CSR requester Operation: CSR read/write/set/clear Object: mstatus, mepc, mcause and performance/control CSRs in zeroriscy_cs_registers Expected guard: CSR address decode and write/read enables must enforce required privilege/CSR access class and prevent unauthorized writes to protected machine/debug/control CSRs Observed behavior: zeroriscy_cs_registers exposes csr_access_i, csr_addr_i, csr_wdata_i and csr_op_i but no privilege input is shown. Lines 200-208 show address-decode writes to mstatus/mepc/mcause when csr_we_int is set. Lines 239-256 derive csr_we_int solely from csr_op_i, disabling only CSR_OP_NONE. Lines 276-299 reset and update mstatus/mepc/mcause; mstatus.mpp is fixed to PRIV_LVL_M. No illegal-instruction/error response or privilege/access-class guard is present in the observations. Path: decoded CSR operation or debug CSR access -> zeroriscy_cs_registers csr_access_i/csr_addr_i/csr_op_i/csr_wdata_i -> CSR write/read muxes Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: If untrusted software can execute CSR instructions accepted by decode/id-stage, it may alter machine interrupt enable and exception return/cause state without privilege mediation in this core configuration; debug CSR path can also write when halted.

findings.summary: CPU data load/store requests are routed through fc_demux based on a single port_sel_i/is_scm_data_req selection and granted to one of two memory buses without observed PMP/security-domain/range-deny checks; address-map constants define regions but no denial response is shown.
findings.vulnerability_category: load/store bus transaction routing / SoC L2/SCM/TCDM/APB/peripheral address regions reachable from core data bus / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: CPU data master Operation: load/store bus transaction routing Object: SoC L2/SCM/TCDM/APB/peripheral address regions reachable from core data bus Expected guard: address-range/PMP/security-domain mediation must be applied consistently to loads and stores before requests are granted/routed to protected memory or peripheral regions Observed behavior: Load/store unit asserts data_req_o directly for data_req_ex_i and waits for data_gnt_i/rvalid; no range/security check appears in the observed FSM. fc_demux copies slave req/add/wen/wdata/be to selected master ports and grants based on selected port; selection is port_sel_i rather than a visible permission decision. soc_bus_defines lists address regions including SOC_L2, SOC_APB, FC_APB, cluster and stdout ranges, but no protected-range deny path is observed. Path: zeroriscy_load_store_unit -> zeroriscy_core data_* -> fc_subsystem core_data_bus/fc_demux_data_i -> l2_data_master or scm_l2_data_master Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: CPU software may issue loads/stores to routed regions without hardware permission mediation in this scope; if any connected region is intended to be protected, both reads and writes could bypass privilege/PMP-style checks.

findings.summary: Instruction fetch requests are generated from PC/fetch FSM and routed through the same fc_demux style path without observed execute/fetch permission, security-mode, or protected-region deny checks.
findings.vulnerability_category: instruction fetch/read routing / instruction memory regions and fetch-enable control / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: CPU instruction fetch unit Operation: instruction fetch/read routing Object: instruction memory regions and fetch-enable control Expected guard: instruction fetch routing to protected/secure/private regions must be mediated by execute/fetch permission or security-mode/address checks Observed behavior: IF stage uses boot_addr_i, req_i, pc_set_i and PC muxing to form fetch_addr/instr_addr_o; observed logic controls validity/stall but not fetch permissions. fc_subsystem instantiates separate instruction bus and fc_demux_instr_i according to fact layer and observed core connection. fc_demux routes requests by port_sel_i with no observed execute/security check. Address constants define memory/peripheral regions but no execute permissions. Path: zeroriscy_if_stage instr_req_o/instr_addr_o -> zeroriscy_core -> fc_subsystem core_instr_bus/fc_demux_instr_i -> l2_instr_master or scm_l2_instr_master Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Instruction fetches can be routed by address/port selection without an execute-permission or security-domain check in the inspected scope; if private/secure/non-executable regions are present, fetch may bypass intended data-side controls.

findings.summary: APB interrupt controller allows APB writes and event inputs to modify interrupt mask/pending/ack state and drive core IRQ outputs with APB pready always true and pslverr always false; no APB privilege/security/event-source authorization guard is observed. fetch_en_o and core_clock_en_o are tied high rather than software-controlled.
findings.vulnerability_category: configure/trigger interrupt state; attempt to alter core control/security outputs / r_mask, r_int, r_ack, r_fifo_event, core_irq_id_o/core_irq_req_o/core_irq_sec_o, fetch_en_o, core_clock_en_o / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: APB software master or event source Operation: configure/trigger interrupt state; attempt to alter core control/security outputs Object: r_mask, r_int, r_ack, r_fifo_event, core_irq_id_o/core_irq_req_o/core_irq_sec_o, fetch_en_o, core_clock_en_o Expected guard: writes or events that alter fetch enable, core clock enable, or secure interrupt classification must be gated by APB access control, privilege/security mode, or trusted event-source authorization Observed behavior: apb_interrupt_cntrl decodes APB writes/reads purely from psel/penable/pwrite and address. Registers r_mask/r_int/r_ack update on APB set/clear/direct writes, event inputs, or core ack. APB pready is 1 and pslverr is 0. No privilege/security authorization input is used for APB writes in the observed code. core_clock_en_o and fetch_en_o are tied to 1'b1, so the specific control-output alteration risk is not present for those outputs in this implementation. Path: APB slave/event_fifo/events_i -> apb_interrupt_cntrl decode/update registers -> core_irq_* outputs -> fc_subsystem/core Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Any APB master reaching the interrupt controller can program mask/pending/ack and potentially trigger/clear CPU interrupts without local authorization checks; denial/error response for unauthorized APB access is absent.

findings.summary: BIST_BUS interface defines bist_en, bist_pattern, bist_done_even/odd, and bist_result_even/odd master/slave modports. apb_interrupt_cntrl has test_mode_i but the observed lines do not show it used for protected-state access. fc_subsystem connects test_en_i to core test_en_i and to fc_hwpe test_mode_i. fc_hwpe passes test_mode_i into mac_top_wrap. No lifecycle/test authorization source or normal-mode isolation logic is shown, but also no complete BIST use-site reaching memory/state is provided.
findings.vulnerability_category: test enable, BIST pattern/result exposure, test-mode clock/test behavior / BIST_BUS signals, test_mode_i/test_en_i paths, possible memories/control interfaces / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: test/BIST interface Operation: test enable, BIST pattern/result exposure, test-mode clock/test behavior Object: BIST_BUS signals, test_mode_i/test_en_i paths, possible memories/control interfaces Expected guard: test/BIST mode must be restricted by lifecycle/test authorization and must not allow unauthenticated read/write/exposure of memory or protected state during normal operation Observed behavior: BIST_BUS interface defines bist_en, bist_pattern, bist_done_even/odd, and bist_result_even/odd master/slave modports. apb_interrupt_cntrl has test_mode_i but the observed lines do not show it used for protected-state access. fc_subsystem connects test_en_i to core test_en_i and to fc_hwpe test_mode_i. fc_hwpe passes test_mode_i into mac_top_wrap. No lifecycle/test authorization source or normal-mode isolation logic is shown, but also no complete BIST use-site reaching memory/state is provided. Path: test_mode_i/test_en_i and BIST_BUS interfaces -> apb_interrupt_cntrl/fc_hwpe/core clock gates/BIST-connected memories Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: Potential risk remains if test_mode_i or BIST_BUS can be asserted externally in normal operation and reach memories/HWPE/core clocking; provided evidence is insufficient to prove an exploitable protected-state read/write/exposure path.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: No provided observation shows the source/trust boundary for test_en_i/test_mode_i, BIST_BUS instantiation, lifecycle authorization, or a concrete test/BIST path to protected arrays/state.

findings.summary: fc_hwpe exposes multiple HW accelerator TCDM master ports and an APB configuration slave; accelerator-generated TCDM requests/addresses/writes are connected to SoC L2 HWPE masters without an observed address-range/security-domain permission guard.
findings.vulnerability_category: accelerator memory read/write and APB-configured control request / shared memory/TCDM/L2 regions via l2_hwpe_master and HWPE configuration/status path / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: HWPE/accelerator master Operation: accelerator memory read/write and APB-configured control request Object: shared memory/TCDM/L2 regions via l2_hwpe_master and HWPE configuration/status path Expected guard: hardware accelerator requests must be mediated by address-range/security-domain checks and must not bypass CPU-side protections or access restricted shared memory/peripheral regions Observed behavior: fc_subsystem instantiates fc_hwpe with l2_hwpe_master array and apb_slave_hwpe. fc_hwpe declares tcdm_req/gnt/add/wen/be/wdata/rdata signals, converts APB config to peripheral request signals with apb2per, and instantiates mac_top_wrap producing TCDM signals. No permission/range/security check is visible in the fc_hwpe observations before requests reach hwacc_xbar_master/l2_hwpe_master. Path: APB hwacc_cfg_slave -> apb2per -> mac_top_wrap control; mac_top_wrap tcdm_req/tcdm_add/tcdm_wen/tcdm_wdata -> hwacc_xbar_master/l2_hwpe_master Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: An APB-configured accelerator may act as an independent memory master and access shared memory regions outside CPU CSR/PMP checks unless upstream fabric enforces protections not shown here.

findings.summary: Secure interrupt/security-mode signaling appears inconsistent: apb_interrupt_cntrl has core_secure_mode_i and core_irq_sec_o, but fc_subsystem connects the core irq_sec_i input to constant 1'b0 and leaves sec_lvl_o unconsumed; no mediated mode-transition or authoritative secure classification path is observed.
findings.vulnerability_category: change/consume security mode or secure interrupt classification / core_secure_mode_i, core_irq_sec_o, core irq_sec_i/sec_lvl_o secure mode/classification path / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: interrupt/event logic and CPU control state Operation: change/consume security mode or secure interrupt classification Object: core_secure_mode_i, core_irq_sec_o, core irq_sec_i/sec_lvl_o secure mode/classification path Expected guard: security-mode state and secure interrupt indications must be generated and consumed consistently, with mode changes mediated and not solely controlled by untrusted interrupt/event inputs Observed behavior: apb_interrupt_cntrl declares core_secure_mode_i input and core_irq_sec_o output. fc_subsystem declares core_irq_sec signal but observed core instantiation at lines 197-202 ties irq_sec_i to 1'b0 and leaves sec_lvl_o unconnected. The provided observations do not show a source for core_secure_mode_i into apb_interrupt_cntrl or consumption of core_irq_sec_o by the core; APB/event interrupt state can be changed without security authorization as noted in PERM-IRQ-001. Path: apb_interrupt_cntrl core_secure_mode_i/core_irq_sec_o <-> fc_subsystem core_irq_sec/core connections -> core irq_sec_i/sec_lvl_o/CSR/controller consumers Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Secure interrupt classification may be ignored or forced non-secure at the core boundary, or security-mode feedback may be disconnected; this can cause misclassification/bypass of intended secure interrupt handling if the SoC relies on these signals.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Full fc_subsystem connections for apb_interrupt_cntrl core_secure_mode_i/core_irq_sec_o and the alternate zero-riscy branch are not fully visible in observations; exact secure-mode semantics are therefore partly uncertain.

global_uncertainty: No closure-level missing evidence was recorded beyond the visible source scope limitations.
